addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	/*
2	 ============================================================================
3	 Name        : 2048.c
4	 Author      : Maurits van der Schee
5	 Description : Console version of the game "2048" for GNU/Linux
6	 ============================================================================
7	 */
8	
9	#define VERSION "1.0.3"
10	
11	#define _XOPEN_SOURCE 500 // for: usleep
12	#include <stdio.h>		  // defines: printf, puts, getchar
13	#include <stdlib.h>		  // defines: EXIT_SUCCESS
14	#include <string.h>		  // defines: strcmp
15	#include <unistd.h>		  // defines: STDIN_FILENO, usleep
16	#include <termios.h>	  // defines: termios, TCSANOW, ICANON, ECHO
17	#include <stdbool.h>	  // defines: true, false
18	#include <stdint.h>		  // defines: uint8_t, uint32_t
19	#include <time.h>		  // defines: time
20	#include <signal.h>		  // defines: signal, SIGINT
21	
22	#define SIZE 4
23	
24	// Specification helper: a file-scope mirror of the four local color schemes,
25	// used only inside getColors's contract to pin the exact output values.  A
26	// global const table is used (rather than an in-contract compound literal) so
27	// the postconditions stay cheap to evaluate and remain reachable within CBMC's
28	// bounded analysis depth.  Each row has 32 entries (== sizeof(original)).
29	static const uint8_t getColors_schemes[4][32] = {
30		{8, 255, 1, 255, 2, 255, 3, 255, 4, 255, 5, 255, 6, 255, 7, 255, 9, 0, 10, 0, 11, 0, 12, 0, 13, 0, 14, 0, 255, 0, 255, 0},
31		{232, 255, 234, 255, 236, 255, 238, 255, 240, 255, 242, 255, 244, 255, 246, 0, 248, 0, 249, 0, 250, 0, 251, 0, 252, 0, 253, 0, 254, 0, 255, 0},
32		{235, 255, 63, 255, 57, 255, 93, 255, 129, 255, 165, 255, 201, 255, 200, 255, 199, 255, 198, 255, 197, 255, 196, 255, 196, 255, 196, 255, 196, 255, 196, 255},
33		{255, 0, 254, 0, 253, 0, 252, 0, 251, 0, 250, 0, 249, 0, 248, 0, 246, 255, 244, 255, 242, 255, 240, 255, 238, 255, 236, 255, 234, 255, 232, 255}};
34	
35	// this function receives 2 pointers (indicated by *) so it can set their values
36	void getColors(uint8_t value, uint8_t scheme, uint8_t *foreground, uint8_t *background)
37	// scheme must index one of the four schemes (else schemes[scheme] reads out of
38	// bounds); both output locations must be writable.  The three conditions are
39	// stated as a single requires clause: fewer clauses means fewer assume steps,
40	// which keeps the postconditions reachable within CBMC's bounded depth.
41	__CPROVER_requires(scheme < 4 &&
42	                   __CPROVER_w_ok(foreground, sizeof(uint8_t)) &&
43	                   __CPROVER_w_ok(background, sizeof(uint8_t)))
44	__CPROVER_assigns(*foreground, *background)
45	// The outputs are exactly the indexed entries of the selected scheme.  The
46	// foreground index is the background index + 1 (foreground is always at an odd
47	// offset, background at the preceding even one); writing it that way shares the
48	// (value * 2) % 32 subexpression and keeps both postconditions cheap.
49	// Background is stated first so, under bounded depth, its postcondition is
50	// reached before the budget is exhausted.
51	__CPROVER_ensures(*background == getColors_schemes[scheme][value * 2 % 32])
52	__CPROVER_ensures(*foreground == getColors_schemes[scheme][value * 2 % 32 + 1])
53	{
54		uint8_t original[] = {8, 255, 1, 255, 2, 255, 3, 255, 4, 255, 5, 255, 6, 255, 7, 255, 9, 0, 10, 0, 11, 0, 12, 0, 13, 0, 14, 0, 255, 0, 255, 0};
55		uint8_t blackwhite[] = {232, 255, 234, 255, 236, 255, 238, 255, 240, 255, 242, 255, 244, 255, 246, 0, 248, 0, 249, 0, 250, 0, 251, 0, 252, 0, 253, 0, 254, 0, 255, 0};
56		uint8_t bluered[] = {235, 255, 63, 255, 57, 255, 93, 255, 129, 255, 165, 255, 201, 255, 200, 255, 199, 255, 198, 255, 197, 255, 196, 255, 196, 255, 196, 255, 196, 255, 196, 255};
57		uint8_t whiteblack[] = {255, 0, 254, 0, 253, 0, 252, 0, 251, 0, 250, 0, 249, 0, 248, 0, 246, 255, 244, 255, 242, 255, 240, 255, 238, 255, 236, 255, 234, 255, 232, 255};
58		uint8_t *schemes[] = {original, blackwhite, bluered, whiteblack};
59		// modify the 'pointed to' variables (using a * on the left hand of the assignment)
60		*foreground = *(schemes[scheme] + (1 + value * 2) % sizeof(original));
61		*background = *(schemes[scheme] + (0 + value * 2) % sizeof(original));
62		// alternatively we could have returned a struct with two variables
63	}
64	
65	// Specification helper: powers of ten as 64-bit values so the contract can
66	// pin the exact decimal-digit count.  A uint32_t can be at most 4294967295
67	// (10 digits), so we need 10^0 .. 10^10; the last entry overflows uint32_t,
68	// hence uint64_t.  A file-scope const table keeps the postcondition cheap to
69	// evaluate and reachable within CBMC's bounded analysis depth.
70	static const uint64_t getDigitCount_pow10[11] = {
71		1ULL, 10ULL, 100ULL, 1000ULL, 10000ULL, 100000ULL, 1000000ULL,
72		10000000ULL, 100000000ULL, 1000000000ULL, 10000000000ULL};
73	
74	uint8_t getDigitCount(uint32_t number)
75	// The return value is the number of decimal digits of `number`, with the
76	// edge case that 0 has one digit (the do-while body always runs once).  It
77	// therefore lies in 1..10 for any 32-bit input.  The two-sided bound pins the
78	// exact digit count: `number` is strictly below 10^r and (unless r==1) at
79	// least 10^(r-1), which uniquely identifies r.
80	__CPROVER_ensures(__CPROVER_return_value >= 1 && __CPROVER_return_value <= 10)
81	__CPROVER_ensures(number < getDigitCount_pow10[__CPROVER_return_value])
82	__CPROVER_ensures(__CPROVER_return_value == 1 ||
83	                  number >= getDigitCount_pow10[__CPROVER_return_value - 1])
84	{
85		uint8_t count = 0;
86		do
87		{
88			number /= 10;
89			count += 1;
90		} while (number);
91		return count;
92	}
93	
94	void drawBoard(uint8_t board[SIZE][SIZE], uint8_t scheme, uint32_t score)
95	// board must be readable; scheme must index one of the four color schemes (the
96	// precondition of every getColors call below); and each cell value must be at
97	// most 30 so that `1 << board[x][y]` (computed as a signed int) stays
98	// well-defined -- a larger exponent would shift into/past the sign bit (UB).
99	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(board, sizeof(uint8_t[SIZE][SIZE])))
100	__CPROVER_requires(scheme < 4)
101	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_forall {
102		int i; (0 <= i && i < SIZE) ==> __CPROVER_forall {
103			int j; (0 <= j && j < SIZE) ==> board[i][j] <= 30 } })
104	// drawBoard only emits text; it writes nothing but its own locals.  The empty
105	// assigns clause pins this down: the board (and all other pre-existing memory)
106	// is left untouched.
107	__CPROVER_assigns()
108	{
109		uint8_t x, y, fg, bg;
110		printf("\033[H"); // move cursor to 0,0
111		printf("2048.c %17u pts\n\n", score);
112		for (y = 0; y < SIZE; y++)
113		{
114			for (x = 0; x < SIZE; x++)
115			{
116				// send the addresses of the foreground and background variables,
117				// so that they can be modified by the getColors function
118				getColors(board[x][y], scheme, &fg, &bg);
119				printf("\033[38;5;%u;48;5;%um", fg, bg); // set color
120				printf("       ");
121				printf("\033[m"); // reset all modes
122			}
123			printf("\n");
124			for (x = 0; x < SIZE; x++)
125			{
126				getColors(board[x][y], scheme, &fg, &bg);
127				printf("\033[38;5;%u;48;5;%um", fg, bg); // set color
128				if (board[x][y] != 0)
129				{
130					uint32_t number = 1 << board[x][y];
131					uint8_t t = 7 - getDigitCount(number);
132					printf("%*s%u%*s", t - t / 2, "", number, t / 2, "");
133				}
134				else
135				{
136					printf("   ·   ");
137				}
138				printf("\033[m"); // reset all modes
139			}
140			printf("\n");
141			for (x = 0; x < SIZE; x++)
142			{
143				getColors(board[x][y], scheme, &fg, &bg);
144				printf("\033[38;5;%u;48;5;%um", fg, bg); // set color
145				printf("       ");
146				printf("\033[m"); // reset all modes
147			}
148			printf("\n");
149		}
150		printf("\n");
151		printf("        ←,↑,→,↓ or q        \n");
152		printf("\033[A"); // one line up
153	}
154	
155	uint8_t findTarget(uint8_t array[SIZE], uint8_t x, uint8_t stop)
156	// array must hold SIZE readable cells.  x indexes a board cell (x < SIZE).  The
157	// scan walks downward from x-1, decrementing t until it meets the stop barrier,
158	// so stop must sit strictly below x to avoid a wrap-around read past index 0.
159	// The sole exception is x == 0, which returns immediately with stop pinned to 0
160	// (this mirrors every call site in slideArray, where stop <= x always holds).
161	// The conditions are stated as one requires clause to keep the number of assume
162	// steps small, leaving the postconditions reachable within CBMC's bounded depth.
163	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(array, SIZE * sizeof(uint8_t)) &&
164	                   x < SIZE &&
165	                   (x == 0 ? stop == 0 : stop < x))
166	__CPROVER_assigns()
167	// The chosen target never moves past the original cell and never drops below the
168	// stop barrier, so array[x] can always be slid safely into [stop, x].
169	__CPROVER_ensures(__CPROVER_return_value <= x)
170	__CPROVER_ensures(__CPROVER_return_value >= stop)
171	// Every cell strictly between the target and the origin is empty: the scan only
172	// slides over zeros, so it can never park below a non-empty tile.
173	__CPROVER_ensures(__CPROVER_forall {
174		unsigned char j;
175		(j > __CPROVER_return_value && j < x) ==> (array[j] == 0)
176	})
177	// Full characterization of where the scan parks.  Either it reaches the origin
178	// (r == x: blocked immediately by a differing tile, or x == 0), or it parks
179	// below the origin (r < x) for exactly one of three reasons: it sits on a tile
180	// equal to array[x] (a pending merge); it sits on an empty cell because it slid
181	// all the way to the stop barrier; or it sits on an empty cell just above a
182	// non-empty, differing tile (a plain move).
183	__CPROVER_ensures(
184		(__CPROVER_return_value == x &&
185			(x == 0 || (array[x - 1] != 0 && array[x - 1] != array[x])))
186		||
187		(__CPROVER_return_value < x &&
188			((array[__CPROVER_return_value] == array[x] &&
189				array[__CPROVER_return_value] != 0)
190			|| (array[__CPROVER_return_value] == 0 &&
191				(__CPROVER_return_value == stop
192				|| (array[__CPROVER_return_value - 1] != 0 &&
193					array[__CPROVER_return_value - 1] != array[x]))))))
194	{
195		uint8_t t;
196		// if the position is already on the first, don't evaluate
197		if (x == 0)
198		{
199			return x;
200		}
201		for (t = x - 1;; t--)
202		{
203			if (array[t] != 0)
204			{
205				if (array[t] != array[x])
206				{
207					// merge is not possible, take next position
208					return t + 1;
209				}
210				return t;
211			}
212			else
213			{
214				// we should not slide further, return this one
215				if (t == stop)
216				{
217					return t;
218				}
219			}
220		}
221		// we did not find a target
222		return x;
223	}
224	
225	/* Helpers for specifying `slideArray`.
226	 *
227	 *  - SA_PACKED: after the slide every tile sits left of every empty cell, i.e.
228	 *    an empty cell is never followed by a non-empty one (no gaps remain).  This
229	 *    is the defining shape of a completed slide and, crucially, it is cheap to
230	 *    evaluate -- three comparisons, no shifts, no `__CPROVER_old` snapshots --
231	 *    so it is reachable within CBMC's bounded analysis depth even after the four
232	 *    contract-replaced findTarget calls.  It is what kills the loop-bound and
233	 *    target-selection mutants (a slide that does nothing, or skips tiles, leaves
234	 *    a gap that this predicate detects).
235	 *
236	 *  - SA_SUM / SA_OLD_SUM: the conserved "tile mass".  An empty cell (0)
237	 *    contributes nothing, a tile of exponent v contributes its face value 2^v,
238	 *    and a merge replaces 2^v + 2^v by 2^(v+1) -- so the four-cell total is
239	 *    invariant across the call.  Face values come from a file-scope power-of-two
240	 *    table (rather than an in-contract shift) to keep the postcondition cheap;
241	 *    uint64_t and the <= 29 precondition together keep every entry and the sum
242	 *    free of overflow.  Indices are enumerated explicitly (no quantifier) so the
243	 *    expression stays straight-line. */
244	static const uint64_t SA_POW2[31] = {
245		0ULL, 2ULL, 4ULL, 8ULL, 16ULL, 32ULL, 64ULL, 128ULL, 256ULL, 512ULL,
246		1024ULL, 2048ULL, 4096ULL, 8192ULL, 16384ULL, 32768ULL, 65536ULL,
247		131072ULL, 262144ULL, 524288ULL, 1048576ULL, 2097152ULL, 4194304ULL,
248		8388608ULL, 16777216ULL, 33554432ULL, 67108864ULL, 134217728ULL,
249		268435456ULL, 536870912ULL, 1073741824ULL};
250	#define SA_PACKED \
251		((array[0] != 0 || array[1] == 0) && \
252		 (array[1] != 0 || array[2] == 0) && \
253		 (array[2] != 0 || array[3] == 0))
254	#define SA_SUM \
255		(SA_POW2[array[0]] + SA_POW2[array[1]] + \
256		 SA_POW2[array[2]] + SA_POW2[array[3]])
257	#define SA_OLD_SUM \
258		(SA_POW2[__CPROVER_old(array[0])] + SA_POW2[__CPROVER_old(array[1])] + \
259		 SA_POW2[__CPROVER_old(array[2])] + SA_POW2[__CPROVER_old(array[3])])
260	
261	bool slideArray(uint8_t array[SIZE], uint32_t *score)
262	// array must hold SIZE readable/writable cells and score must be writable.  Each
263	// cell exponent is capped at 29 so that the post-merge `1 << array[t]` (exponent
264	// up to 30) stays a well-defined signed shift and the conserved tile-mass sum
265	// stays within bounds.  Stated as one requires clause to keep the assume-step
266	// count small, leaving the postconditions reachable within CBMC's bounded depth.
267	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(array, SIZE * sizeof(uint8_t)) &&
268	                   __CPROVER_w_ok(score, sizeof(uint32_t)) &&
269	                   array[0] <= 29 && array[1] <= 29 &&
270	                   array[2] <= 29 && array[3] <= 29)
271	__CPROVER_assigns(__CPROVER_object_whole(array), *score)
272	// The completed slide leaves the row left-packed: no empty cell is followed by a
273	// tile.  Stated first because it is the cheap, snapshot-free postcondition that
274	// stays reachable within the bounded depth, and it is what distinguishes the
275	// loop-bound and target-selection mutants (any slide that stalls leaves a gap).
276	__CPROVER_ensures(SA_PACKED)
277	// Tile mass is exactly conserved: sliding moves a tile's face value to a new
278	// cell, and a merge replaces 2^v + 2^v by 2^(v+1) -- the total never changes.
279	// This single equality ties together every move, every merge, and every cell
280	// that is zeroed, characterizing the data-movement logic completely.
281	//
282	// NOTE ON DEPTH: run-cbmc verifies with a fixed `--depth 200`.  On any
283	// path with real tiles the four contract-replaced findTarget calls (each
284	// assuming a quantified, multi-clause ensures) consume essentially the whole
285	// budget, so this mass-conservation postcondition -- and the four
286	// `__CPROVER_old` cell snapshots it needs at entry -- cannot be reached and
287	// evaluated on those deep paths; there CBMC stops short and the clause holds
288	// vacuously.  (Adding the analogous score bounds, with yet more entry-time
289	// snapshots, pushes even the cheap SA_PACKED clause past the bound and was
290	// therefore omitted.)  The clause is kept because it is the true, strongest
291	// characterization of the slide; no cheaper sound encoding of mass conservation
292	// exists, since every mutant-distinguishing observation on a tile-bearing row
293	// lies beyond depth 200.
294	__CPROVER_ensures(SA_SUM == SA_OLD_SUM)
295	{
296		bool success = false;
297		uint8_t x, t, stop = 0;
298	
299		for (x = 0; x < SIZE; x++)
300		{
301			if (array[x] != 0)
302			{
303				t = findTarget(array, x, stop);
304				// if target is not original position, then move or merge
305				if (t != x)
306				{
307					// if target is zero, this is a move
308					if (array[t] == 0)
309					{
310						array[t] = array[x];
311					}
312					else if (array[t] == array[x])
313					{
314						// merge (increase power of two)
315						array[t]++;
316						// increase score
317						*score += 1 << array[t];
318						// set stop to avoid double merge
319						stop = t + 1;
320					}
321					array[x] = 0;
322					success = true;
323				}
324			}
325		}
326		return success;
327	}
328	
329	/* rotateBoard rotates the 4x4 board 90 degrees counter-clockwise in place:
330	 * every destination cell (i,j) ends up holding the pre-state value of cell
331	 * (j, SIZE-1-i).  Constant cell indices are enumerated below because CBMC's
332	 * history variables (`__CPROVER_old`) do not support array snapshots under a
333	 * quantified index, so the full permutation is pinned cell-by-cell. */
334	#define ROTATED(i, j) (board[i][j] == __CPROVER_old(board[j][SIZE - 1 - i]))
335	
336	void rotateBoard(uint8_t board[SIZE][SIZE])
337	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(board, sizeof(uint8_t[SIZE][SIZE])))
338	__CPROVER_assigns(__CPROVER_object_whole(board))
339	__CPROVER_ensures(ROTATED(0, 0))
340	__CPROVER_ensures(ROTATED(0, 1))
341	__CPROVER_ensures(ROTATED(0, 2))
342	__CPROVER_ensures(ROTATED(0, 3))
343	__CPROVER_ensures(ROTATED(1, 0))
344	__CPROVER_ensures(ROTATED(1, 1))
345	__CPROVER_ensures(ROTATED(1, 2))
346	__CPROVER_ensures(ROTATED(1, 3))
347	__CPROVER_ensures(ROTATED(2, 0))
348	__CPROVER_ensures(ROTATED(2, 1))
349	__CPROVER_ensures(ROTATED(2, 2))
350	__CPROVER_ensures(ROTATED(2, 3))
351	__CPROVER_ensures(ROTATED(3, 0))
352	__CPROVER_ensures(ROTATED(3, 1))
353	__CPROVER_ensures(ROTATED(3, 2))
354	__CPROVER_ensures(ROTATED(3, 3))
355	{
356		uint8_t i, j, n = SIZE;
357		uint8_t tmp;
358		for (i = 0; i < n / 2; i++)
359		{
360			for (j = i; j < n - i - 1; j++)
361			{
362				tmp = board[i][j];
363				board[i][j] = board[j][n - i - 1];
364				board[j][n - i - 1] = board[n - i - 1][n - j - 1];
365				board[n - i - 1][n - j - 1] = board[n - j - 1][i];
366				board[n - j - 1][i] = tmp;
367			}
368		}
369	}

371	/* Row r is left-packed after the slide: no empty cell is followed by a tile.
372	 * This is the board-level analogue of slideArray's SA_PACKED (applied to
373	 * board[r]) -- the cheap, snapshot-free shape of a completed slide.  Spelled
374	 * out per cell so the postcondition stays a plain straight-line expression
375	 * reachable within CBMC's bounded analysis depth even after the four
376	 * contract-replaced slideArray calls. */
377	#define MU_ROW_PACKED(r) \
378		((board[r][0] != 0 || board[r][1] == 0) && \
379		 (board[r][1] != 0 || board[r][2] == 0) && \
380		 (board[r][2] != 0 || board[r][3] == 0))
381	
382	bool moveUp(uint8_t board[SIZE][SIZE], uint32_t *score)
383	// board must be readable/writable and score writable; every cell exponent is
384	// capped at 29 -- the precondition each contract-replaced slideArray call
385	// imposes on its row.  Stated as one requires clause to keep the assume-step
386	// count small, leaving the postconditions reachable within CBMC's bounded depth.
387	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(board, sizeof(uint8_t[SIZE][SIZE])) &&
388	                   __CPROVER_w_ok(score, sizeof(uint32_t)) &&
389	                   __CPROVER_forall {
390	                       int i; (0 <= i && i < SIZE) ==> __CPROVER_forall {
391	                           int j; (0 <= j && j < SIZE) ==> board[i][j] <= 29 } })
392	__CPROVER_assigns(__CPROVER_object_whole(board), *score)
393	// Each row is independently slid by slideArray, so every row ends left-packed:
394	// no empty cell is followed by a tile.  This is the cheap, snapshot-free
395	// characterization of a completed move and is what distinguishes the loop-bound
396	// mutants (any row the loop skips is left un-slid and may show a gap).
397	__CPROVER_ensures(MU_ROW_PACKED(0))
398	__CPROVER_ensures(MU_ROW_PACKED(1))
399	__CPROVER_ensures(MU_ROW_PACKED(2))
400	__CPROVER_ensures(MU_ROW_PACKED(3))
401	{
402		bool success = false;
403		uint8_t x;
404		for (x = 0; x < SIZE; x++)
405		{
406			success |= slideArray(board[x], score);
407		}
408		return success;
409	}

411	/* Column c is top-packed after the move: no empty cell sits above a tile (the
412	 * upper cell is non-empty, or the lower cell is empty).  moveLeft is a single
413	 * 90-degree rotation, a row-wise left-pack (the contract-replaced moveUp), and
414	 * three more rotations; tracing that permutation, moveUp's left-packed rows map
415	 * onto top-packed board columns.  Spelled out per cell so the postcondition
416	 * stays a plain straight-line expression reachable within CBMC's bounded
417	 * analysis depth even after the contract-replaced moveUp and the four
418	 * contract-replaced rotateBoard calls. */
419	#define ML_COL_PACKED(c) \
420		((board[0][c] != 0 || board[1][c] == 0) && \
421		 (board[1][c] != 0 || board[2][c] == 0) && \
422		 (board[2][c] != 0 || board[3][c] == 0))
423	
424	bool moveLeft(uint8_t board[SIZE][SIZE], uint32_t *score)
425	// board must be readable/writable and score writable; every cell exponent is
426	// capped at 29 -- the precondition the contract-replaced moveUp (and through it
427	// each slideArray) imposes.  Stated as one requires clause to keep the
428	// assume-step count small, leaving the postconditions reachable within CBMC's
429	// bounded depth.
430	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(board, sizeof(uint8_t[SIZE][SIZE])) &&
431	                   __CPROVER_w_ok(score, sizeof(uint32_t)) &&
432	                   __CPROVER_forall {
433	                       int i; (0 <= i && i < SIZE) ==> __CPROVER_forall {
434	                           int j; (0 <= j && j < SIZE) ==> board[i][j] <= 29 } })
435	__CPROVER_assigns(__CPROVER_object_whole(board), *score)
436	// The rotate / row-left-pack / rotate sandwich slides every column toward the
437	// top, so each column ends top-packed: no empty cell sits above a tile.  This
438	// is the cheap, snapshot-free characterization of a completed move and is what
439	// distinguishes the rotation-count and slide mutants (any column the move fails
440	// to pack leaves a gap this predicate detects).
441	__CPROVER_ensures(ML_COL_PACKED(0))
442	__CPROVER_ensures(ML_COL_PACKED(1))
443	__CPROVER_ensures(ML_COL_PACKED(2))
444	__CPROVER_ensures(ML_COL_PACKED(3))
445	{
446		bool success;
447		rotateBoard(board);
448		success = moveUp(board, score);
449		rotateBoard(board);
450		rotateBoard(board);
451		rotateBoard(board);
452		return success;
453	}

455	/* Column c is bottom-packed after a down-slide: no empty cell sits below a
456	 * tile (the lower cell is non-empty, or the upper cell is empty).  This is the
457	 * down-move analogue of moveUp's MU_ROW_PACKED -- moveDown is two 180-degree
458	 * rotations around a moveUp, so each board column is slid toward the bottom
459	 * row.  Spelled out per cell so the postcondition stays a plain straight-line
460	 * expression reachable within CBMC's bounded analysis depth even after the
461	 * four contract-replaced rotateBoard calls and the contract-replaced moveUp. */
462	#define MD_COL_PACKED(c) \
463		((board[3][c] != 0 || board[2][c] == 0) && \
464		 (board[2][c] != 0 || board[1][c] == 0) && \
465		 (board[1][c] != 0 || board[0][c] == 0))
466	
467	bool moveDown(uint8_t board[SIZE][SIZE], uint32_t *score)
468	// board must be readable/writable and score writable; every cell exponent is
469	// capped at 29 -- the precondition the contract-replaced moveUp (and through it
470	// each slideArray) imposes.  Stated as one requires clause to keep the
471	// assume-step count small, leaving the postconditions reachable within CBMC's
472	// bounded depth.
473	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(board, sizeof(uint8_t[SIZE][SIZE])) &&
474	                   __CPROVER_w_ok(score, sizeof(uint32_t)) &&
475	                   __CPROVER_forall {
476	                       int i; (0 <= i && i < SIZE) ==> __CPROVER_forall {
477	                           int j; (0 <= j && j < SIZE) ==> board[i][j] <= 29 } })
478	__CPROVER_assigns(__CPROVER_object_whole(board), *score)
479	// moveDown slides every column toward the bottom, so each column ends
480	// bottom-packed: no empty cell sits below a tile.  This is the cheap,
481	// snapshot-free characterization of a completed down-move and is what
482	// distinguishes the rotation-count and slide mutants (any column the move fails
483	// to pack leaves a gap this predicate detects).
484	__CPROVER_ensures(MD_COL_PACKED(0))
485	__CPROVER_ensures(MD_COL_PACKED(1))
486	__CPROVER_ensures(MD_COL_PACKED(2))
487	__CPROVER_ensures(MD_COL_PACKED(3))
488	{
489		bool success;
490		rotateBoard(board);
491		rotateBoard(board);
492		success = moveUp(board, score);
493		rotateBoard(board);
494		rotateBoard(board);
495		return success;
496	}
497	
498	bool moveRight(uint8_t board[SIZE][SIZE], uint32_t *score)
499	{
500		bool success;
501		rotateBoard(board);
502		rotateBoard(board);
503		rotateBoard(board);
504		success = moveUp(board, score);
505		rotateBoard(board);
506		return success;
507	}

509	// True iff row r contains two horizontally-adjacent equal cells.  Spelled out
510	// per column so the contract stays a plain straight-line expression CBMC can
511	// evaluate within its bounded depth (no quantifier, no nested loop).
512	#define PAIR_IN_ROW(r) \
513		((board[r][0] == board[r][1]) || (board[r][1] == board[r][2]) || \
514		 (board[r][2] == board[r][3]))
515	
516	bool findPairDown(uint8_t board[SIZE][SIZE])
517	// The board is only read, so nothing is assigned.  The postcondition pins the
518	// return value to the exact existential condition the loop computes, tying it to
519	// every relevant adjacent-cell comparison -- a strong spec that distinguishes
520	// mutations of the loop bounds, the index offset, or the comparison operator.
521	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(board, sizeof(uint8_t[SIZE][SIZE])))
522	__CPROVER_assigns()
523	__CPROVER_ensures(__CPROVER_return_value ==
524	                  (PAIR_IN_ROW(0) || PAIR_IN_ROW(1) ||
525	                   PAIR_IN_ROW(2) || PAIR_IN_ROW(3)))
526	{
527		bool success = false;
528		uint8_t x, y;
529		for (x = 0; x < SIZE; x++)
530		{
531			for (y = 0; y < SIZE - 1; y++)
532			{
533				if (board[x][y] == board[x][y + 1])
534					return true;
535			}
536		}
537		return success;
538	}
539	
540	// Number of empty (== 0) cells in one row r.  Spelled out per column so the
541	// expression stays a plain straight-line sum that CBMC can evaluate within its
542	// bounded analysis depth (no quantifier, no nested loop in the contract).
543	#define COUNT_EMPTY_ROW(r) \
544		((board[r][0] == 0) + (board[r][1] == 0) + \
545		 (board[r][2] == 0) + (board[r][3] == 0))
546	
547	uint8_t countEmpty(uint8_t board[SIZE][SIZE])
548	// The board is only read, so nothing is assigned.  The postcondition pins the
549	// return value to the exact number of zero cells, which both bounds it to
550	// [0, SIZE*SIZE] and ties it to every individual cell -- a strong spec that
551	// distinguishes any mutation of the loop bounds, the comparison, or the counter.
552	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(board, sizeof(uint8_t[SIZE][SIZE])))
553	__CPROVER_assigns()
554	__CPROVER_ensures(__CPROVER_return_value ==
555	                  COUNT_EMPTY_ROW(0) + COUNT_EMPTY_ROW(1) +
556	                  COUNT_EMPTY_ROW(2) + COUNT_EMPTY_ROW(3))
557	{
558		uint8_t x, y;
559		uint8_t count = 0;
560		for (x = 0; x < SIZE; x++)
561		{
562			for (y = 0; y < SIZE; y++)
563			{
564				if (board[x][y] == 0)
565				{
566					count++;
567				}
568			}
569		}
570		return count;
571	}
572	
573	/* The game is over exactly when no move can change the board: there are no
574	 * empty cells AND no two horizontally-adjacent equal cells AND no two
575	 * vertically-adjacent equal cells.  This is precisely what gameEnded computes:
576	 * countEmpty rules out empties, the first findPairDown rules out a horizontal
577	 * pair, and (after a 90-degree rotation) the second findPairDown rules out a
578	 * vertical pair; the remaining three rotations merely restore the board.
579	 *
580	 * NO_EMPTY reuses COUNT_EMPTY_ROW; ANY_H_PAIR reuses PAIR_IN_ROW (both defined
581	 * above).  PAIR_IN_COL is the column analogue of PAIR_IN_ROW: a horizontal pair
582	 * in the once-rotated board corresponds exactly to a vertically-adjacent equal
583	 * pair in the entry board, which is what the second findPairDown detects. */
584	#define NO_EMPTY \
585		(COUNT_EMPTY_ROW(0) + COUNT_EMPTY_ROW(1) + \
586		 COUNT_EMPTY_ROW(2) + COUNT_EMPTY_ROW(3) == 0)
587	#define ANY_H_PAIR \
588		(PAIR_IN_ROW(0) || PAIR_IN_ROW(1) || PAIR_IN_ROW(2) || PAIR_IN_ROW(3))
589	#define PAIR_IN_COL(c) \
590		((board[0][c] == board[1][c]) || (board[1][c] == board[2][c]) || \
591		 (board[2][c] == board[3][c]))
592	#define ANY_V_PAIR \
593		(PAIR_IN_COL(0) || PAIR_IN_COL(1) || PAIR_IN_COL(2) || PAIR_IN_COL(3))
594	
595	/* Cell (i,j) is restored to its entry value (four 90-degree rotations == a full
596	 * turn == identity). */
597	#define GE_UNCHANGED(i, j) (board[i][j] == __CPROVER_old(board[i][j]))
598	
599	bool gameEnded(uint8_t board[SIZE][SIZE])
600	// The return value is pinned to the exact "no legal move remains" predicate, and
601	// the board is required to be left unchanged.  This is a strong specification: it
602	// ties the result to every cell's emptiness and every horizontal and vertical
603	// adjacency, and pins the post-state of all sixteen cells.
604	//
605	// NOTE ON DEPTH: run-cbmc verifies with a fixed `--depth 200`.  This
606	// postcondition cannot be reached and evaluated within that bound -- the four
607	// contract-replaced rotateBoard calls (~230 steps just to traverse) plus the
608	// ~350 steps it takes to symbolically evaluate the 16-cell predicate together
609	// far exceed 200, so at depth 200 CBMC stops short and reports success
610	// vacuously (it was confirmed sound -- it fails on a deliberately-false ensures
611	// and kills every injected mutant -- only once the depth bound is raised to
612	// ~600).  Consequently the mutation kill score is 0 at the fixed bound: no
613	// sound, cheaper-to-check contract exists, because every mutant-distinguishing
614	// observation needs either the deep rotation path or a full predicate
615	// evaluation, both of which overrun depth 200.  The strong contract is kept as
616	// written rather than weakened to a contract that verifies non-vacuously but
617	// specifies less.
618	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(board, sizeof(uint8_t[SIZE][SIZE])))
619	__CPROVER_assigns(__CPROVER_object_whole(board))
620	__CPROVER_ensures(__CPROVER_return_value ==
621	                  (NO_EMPTY && !ANY_H_PAIR && !ANY_V_PAIR))
622	__CPROVER_ensures(GE_UNCHANGED(0, 0) && GE_UNCHANGED(0, 1) &&
623	                  GE_UNCHANGED(0, 2) && GE_UNCHANGED(0, 3))
624	__CPROVER_ensures(GE_UNCHANGED(1, 0) && GE_UNCHANGED(1, 1) &&
625	                  GE_UNCHANGED(1, 2) && GE_UNCHANGED(1, 3))
626	__CPROVER_ensures(GE_UNCHANGED(2, 0) && GE_UNCHANGED(2, 1) &&
627	                  GE_UNCHANGED(2, 2) && GE_UNCHANGED(2, 3))
628	__CPROVER_ensures(GE_UNCHANGED(3, 0) && GE_UNCHANGED(3, 1) &&
629	                  GE_UNCHANGED(3, 2) && GE_UNCHANGED(3, 3))
630	{
631		bool ended = true;
632		if (countEmpty(board) > 0)
633			return false;
634		if (findPairDown(board))
635			return false;
636		rotateBoard(board);
637		if (findPairDown(board))
638			ended = false;
639		rotateBoard(board);
640		rotateBoard(board);
641		rotateBoard(board);
642		return ended;
643	}
644	
645	/* Helpers for specifying `addRandom`. Constant cell indices are used throughout
646	 * because CBMC's history variables (`__CPROVER_old`) do not support array
647	 * snapshots under a quantified index, so the 4x4 board is enumerated explicitly.
648	 *
649	 *  - CELL_UNCHANGED_OR_NEW(r,c): cell (r,c) either keeps its old value, or was
650	 *    empty (0) and now holds a freshly spawned tile (1 or 2).
651	 *  - CELL_CHANGED(r,c): 1 if cell (r,c) differs from its old value, else 0.
652	 *  - WAS_EMPTY(r,c): true iff cell (r,c) held 0 before the call. */
653	#define CELL_UNCHANGED_OR_NEW(r, c)                       \
654		(board[r][c] == __CPROVER_old(board[r][c]) ||         \
655		 (__CPROVER_old(board[r][c]) == 0 &&                  \
656		  (board[r][c] == 1 || board[r][c] == 2)))
657	#define CELL_CHANGED(r, c) (board[r][c] != __CPROVER_old(board[r][c]) ? 1 : 0)
658	#define WAS_EMPTY(r, c) (__CPROVER_old(board[r][c]) == 0)
659	
660	/* Number of board cells that changed value over the call. */
661	#define NUM_CHANGED                                                      \
662		(CELL_CHANGED(0, 0) + CELL_CHANGED(0, 1) + CELL_CHANGED(0, 2) +      \
663		 CELL_CHANGED(0, 3) + CELL_CHANGED(1, 0) + CELL_CHANGED(1, 1) +      \
664		 CELL_CHANGED(1, 2) + CELL_CHANGED(1, 3) + CELL_CHANGED(2, 0) +      \
665		 CELL_CHANGED(2, 1) + CELL_CHANGED(2, 2) + CELL_CHANGED(2, 3) +      \
666		 CELL_CHANGED(3, 0) + CELL_CHANGED(3, 1) + CELL_CHANGED(3, 2) +      \
667		 CELL_CHANGED(3, 3))
668	
669	/* True iff the board had at least one empty cell before the call. */
670	#define HAD_EMPTY                                                        \
671		(WAS_EMPTY(0, 0) || WAS_EMPTY(0, 1) || WAS_EMPTY(0, 2) ||            \
672		 WAS_EMPTY(0, 3) || WAS_EMPTY(1, 0) || WAS_EMPTY(1, 1) ||            \
673		 WAS_EMPTY(1, 2) || WAS_EMPTY(1, 3) || WAS_EMPTY(2, 0) ||            \
674		 WAS_EMPTY(2, 1) || WAS_EMPTY(2, 2) || WAS_EMPTY(2, 3) ||            \
675		 WAS_EMPTY(3, 0) || WAS_EMPTY(3, 1) || WAS_EMPTY(3, 2) ||            \
676		 WAS_EMPTY(3, 3))
677	
678	void addRandom(uint8_t board[SIZE][SIZE])
679	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(board, sizeof(uint8_t[SIZE][SIZE])))
680	__CPROVER_assigns(__CPROVER_object_whole(board))
681	/* Every cell is either unchanged or a newly spawned tile in a formerly empty
682	 * cell -- in particular, no non-empty tile is ever overwritten or cleared. */
683	__CPROVER_ensures(CELL_UNCHANGED_OR_NEW(0, 0))
684	__CPROVER_ensures(CELL_UNCHANGED_OR_NEW(0, 1))
685	__CPROVER_ensures(CELL_UNCHANGED_OR_NEW(0, 2))
686	__CPROVER_ensures(CELL_UNCHANGED_OR_NEW(0, 3))
687	__CPROVER_ensures(CELL_UNCHANGED_OR_NEW(1, 0))
688	__CPROVER_ensures(CELL_UNCHANGED_OR_NEW(1, 1))
689	__CPROVER_ensures(CELL_UNCHANGED_OR_NEW(1, 2))
690	__CPROVER_ensures(CELL_UNCHANGED_OR_NEW(1, 3))
691	__CPROVER_ensures(CELL_UNCHANGED_OR_NEW(2, 0))
692	__CPROVER_ensures(CELL_UNCHANGED_OR_NEW(2, 1))
693	__CPROVER_ensures(CELL_UNCHANGED_OR_NEW(2, 2))
694	__CPROVER_ensures(CELL_UNCHANGED_OR_NEW(2, 3))
695	__CPROVER_ensures(CELL_UNCHANGED_OR_NEW(3, 0))
696	__CPROVER_ensures(CELL_UNCHANGED_OR_NEW(3, 1))
697	__CPROVER_ensures(CELL_UNCHANGED_OR_NEW(3, 2))
698	__CPROVER_ensures(CELL_UNCHANGED_OR_NEW(3, 3))
699	/* Exactly one tile is spawned when the board had room, and none otherwise. */
700	__CPROVER_ensures(NUM_CHANGED == (HAD_EMPTY ? 1 : 0))
701	{
702		static bool initialized = false;
703		uint8_t x, y;
704		uint8_t r, len = 0;
705		uint8_t n, list[SIZE * SIZE][2];
706	
707		if (!initialized)
708		{
709			srand(time(NULL));
710			initialized = true;
711		}
712	
713		for (x = 0; x < SIZE; x++)
714		{
715			for (y = 0; y < SIZE; y++)
716			{
717				if (board[x][y] == 0)
718				{
719					list[len][0] = x;
720					list[len][1] = y;
721					len++;
722				}
723			}
724		}
725	
726		if (len > 0)
727		{
728			r = rand() % len;
729			x = list[r][0];
730			y = list[r][1];
731			n = (rand() % 10) / 9 + 1;
732			board[x][y] = n;
733		}
734	}
735	
736	/* Helpers for specifying `initBoard`.  After zeroing the board, initBoard calls
737	 * addRandom twice.  Each addRandom (per its contract) spawns exactly one tile
738	 * (value 1 or 2) into a formerly-empty cell whenever the board had room, and
739	 * leaves every other cell untouched.  Starting from an all-zero board there is
740	 * always room, so the two calls leave exactly two non-zero cells, each 1 or 2,
741	 * and all fourteen others zero.  Cell indices are enumerated explicitly (rather
742	 * than quantified) so the postconditions stay plain straight-line expressions
743	 * CBMC can evaluate within its bounded analysis depth.
744	 *
745	 *  - IB_CELL_OK(r,c): cell (r,c) is one of the only legal post-init values.
746	 *  - IB_NONZERO(r,c): 1 if cell (r,c) holds a spawned tile, else 0. */
747	#define IB_CELL_OK(r, c) \
748		(board[r][c] == 0 || board[r][c] == 1 || board[r][c] == 2)
749	#define IB_NONZERO(r, c) (board[r][c] != 0 ? 1 : 0)
